title: Java Unnamed Variables
description: Practice underscore variables that intentionally discard a value.
learningGoals: Recall that `_` declares an unnamed variable when the value is intentionally unused.
Recognize that unnamed variables cannot be read later because they do not bind a name.

completionChecks.prompt: What does seen hold after this code runs?
choices: Compilation fails because `_` cannot be reused as a name
completionChecks.explanation: The loop increments seen to 2, then Integer.parseInt("bad") throws NumberFormatException and the catch sets seen to -1; multiple unnamed `_` variables are allowed because none can be read.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens if code later tries to read `_` from this lesson?
choices: It reads the most recent discarded value
It fails to compile because `_` is unnamed and binds no readable name
completionChecks.explanation: The underscore declares an unnamed variable that discards its value and creates no readable binding, so referring to `_` does not compile.

completionChecks.prompt: Does this compile if the catch block contains System.out.println(_);?
choices: No, _ is an unnamed variable and cannot be read
No, catch clauses cannot use unnamed variables
completionChecks.explanation: The underscore marks the exception parameter as intentionally discarded, so code cannot read it inside the catch block.
